coredata.dc:title: Novel preparation of red fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline sensing and its application in trace determination
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bingyan
Gu, Changxin
Jiao, Yuan
Gao, Yifang
Liu, Xiaona
Guo, Junmei
Qian, Tianwei
coredata.dc:description: The controllable design of red-emitting carbon dots and further exploration of their application in the trace determination of environmental pollutants remains a tremendous challenge. Herein, the novel strategy for red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) with a higher quantum yield of 58.9% was proposed by doping small-molecule urea into the bio-dye of resazurin for the first time, which can retain the luminophore of precursors and exhibit exceptional optical, advantageous reversibility and outstanding photostability. Importantly, the R-CDs exhibit a remarkable fluorescence reduction towards tetracyclines (TCs) accompanied by a noticeable color change of R-CDs solution from red to yellow, which can realize the trace detection of TCs at strelatively low levels, including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC). The linear range of TC, CTC, and OTC are 3–40 μM, 4–50 μM, and 2–50 μM, and the corresponding detection limits are 38.5 nM, 64.6 nM, and 45.4 nM, respectively (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the R-CDs demonstrate sensitivity to the physiological pH in the linear range of 4.0–5.0 and 5.0–6.2 with a pKa of 5.61. As a multifunctional fluorescent sensor, R-CDs can provide a new perspective for the preparation of long-wavelength CDs, and further realize the trace determination of environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-wavelength emission
Carbon dots
Tetracyclines
pH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TCs in real samples
Fluorescence response of R-CDs towards TCs
Discussion of the mechanism of R-CDs detection TCs
Recovery of TCS in milk samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of R-CDs, inset right: HR-TEM of R-CDs, left inset: size distribution of R-CDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of R-CDs. (c) XPS full-scan spectrum of R-CDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s, and (f) O1s of R-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of R-CDs, inset top: the photo of R-CDs under daylight and UV light, respectively. (b) Excitation-emission spectra of R-CDs. (c) Chromaticity color coordinates of R-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The changes in fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at 608 nm in the presence of different antibiotics. (The concentrations of antibiotics are 50 μM).

(a–c) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs solution on adding different concentrations of TCs including TC (0–150 μM), CTC (0–140 μM), and OTC (0–140 μM). The inset in the top site of Fig. 4a–c are the photos of R-CDs with the absence and presence of TCs under the daylight and UV light, respectively. (d–f) The relationship between ΔF and the concentrations of (d) TC, (e) CTC, and (f) OTC.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs at different pH in the range of 2–13 under excitation at 365 nm, inset: the color of the R-CDs solution changed photo with increasing pH under sunlight and 365 nm UV light irradiation. (b) Boltzman function of the fluorescence intensity at 608 nm. (c–d) The linear relationship graph between the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at 608 nm with the pH range of 4–5 and 5–6.2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Fluorescence intensity of R-CDs varies with time under UV light (365 nm）at pH of 3.4, 5.6, and 7.4. (b) The changes in fluorescence intensity of R-CDs for repeated cycling between pH of 2 and 7.
Schematic illustration of R-CDs as multifunctional fluorescent sensors against TCs and pH.
The comparison of different methods for TCs analysis.
The comparation of various fluorescence detection methods for TCs.

head.ce:title: Novel preparation of red fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline sensing and its application in trace determination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong, 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The controllable design of red-emitting carbon dots and further exploration of their application in the trace determination of environmental pollutants remains a tremendous challenge. Herein, the novel strategy for red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) with a higher quantum yield of 58.9% was proposed by doping small-molecule urea into the bio-dye of resazurin for the first time, which can retain the luminophore of precursors and exhibit exceptional optical, advantageous reversibility and outstanding photostability. Importantly, the R-CDs exhibit a remarkable fluorescence reduction towards tetracyclines (TCs) accompanied by a noticeable color change of R-CDs solution from red to yellow, which can realize the trace detection of TCs at strelatively low levels, including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC). The linear range of TC, CTC, and OTC are 3–40 μM, 4–50 μM, and 2–50 μM, and the corresponding detection limits are 38.5 nM, 64.6 nM, and 45.4 nM, respectively (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the R-CDs demonstrate sensitivity to the physiological pH in the linear range of 4.0–5.0 and 5.0–6.2 with a pKa of 5.61. As a multifunctional fluorescent sensor, R-CDs can provide a new perspective for the preparation of long-wavelength CDs, and further realize the trace determination of environmental pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasingly frequent use of antibiotics, the contamination of drinking water caused by antibiotics has aroused worldwide concern [1–3]. Especially, tetracyclines (TCs) have been widely employed in the medical industry as a broad-spectrum antibiotic [4,5] owing to their important role in inhibiting microorganisms. However, only a small amount of TCs can be effectively absorbed by the human body and most of them will be released into the environment accompanied by metabolites [6,7]. The presence of TCs at very low concentrations in the environment can induce the production of resistance genes, thereby causing some health problems such as joint disease, allergic reactions, yellowing of teeth, etc [8,9]. So far, several analytical methods have been established for the quantitative detection of TCs such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometric (LC-MS) [11], enzyme-linked immuno-sorbent assay (ELISA) [12], electrochemical [13] and capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometric (CE-MS) [14], etc. However, these methods may suffer from drawbacks such as requiring sample preparation before analysis, long time, and high cost, which hinder the application of TCs detection. Therefore, it is of great urgency to develop a simple and effective method for trace detection of TCs in drinking water.

As a new type of functional material, carbon-based nanomaterials have been widely explored in various fields because of their excellent physical and electrical properties, such as clean energy [15,16], high-sensitivity sensors [17–19], and environmental governance [20–22], etc. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive attention in the field of environmental pollutants monitoring [23,24] based on their superior optical characteristics, excellent photostability, and low toxicity. Because of the advanced fluorescence performance, CDs have been intensively utilized in specific recognition of target pollutants in drinking water such as anions [25,26], metal ions [26–29], amino acid [30], and organic pollutants [31,32]. of course, the related detection of TCs based on CDs has also been reported in the literature. For instance, Feng et al. [33] obtained blue fluorescent CDs choosing rose flowers as the carbon source for the first time, which were employed for the determination of TC residues in human urine samples. However, most detection methods are based on the response of a single fluorescent group, which ignores the influence of environmental factors such as instrument background and testing process. Hence, Zhi et al. [34] constructed ratiometric fluorescent CDs with a rapid carbonization process, which realized the sensitive response to TC by taking the blue emission of the CDs at 442 nm and the externally grafted lanthanide Eu3+ complex in 616 nm served as a fluorescent internal standard and a response signal, respectively. Miao et al. [35] prepared the bright-blue CDs with the high quantum yield to distinguish three TCs through the test strip and analyze the different mechanisms of CDs response to three TCs. Nevertheless, the fluorescence based on these CDs sensors are limited to blue or green regions with excitation-dependent properties, which restrict their utilization in environmental detection because of fluorescence interference from pollutants and serious photodamage to an environmental living creature. Thus, it is of considerable importance to develop fluorescent CDs with long wavelength emission and further construct a high-sensitivity detection method for sensitive recognizing of TCs.

In this regard, we synthesized R-CDs with red-emitting fluorescence by doping small-molecule urea into the bio-dye of resazurin for the first time. Surprisingly, the prepared R-CDs retain the luminophore of resazurin and exhibit excitation wavelength independence emission with 58.9% as the fluorescence quantum yield. In addition, the R-CDs also hold some other exceptional characteristics such as exceptional optical, advantageous reversibility, and outstanding photostability, which can specifically recognize TCs accompanied by distinct color changes from red to yellow. As well as, the synthesized R-CDs exhibit obvious fluorescence changes under different pH, which is promising to realize the pH detection to predict water quality. The produced R-CDs are demonstrated as a promising nanomaterial for the construction of visual detection platform for sensitive sensing of TCs and pH, which offer a new approach to trace detection of environmental pollutants (Scheme 1 ).

Resazurin was obtained from Shanghai Acmec Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Urea was bought from Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Manufacturing Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). TCs including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chlortetracycline (CTC) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ethanol absolute (EtOH) was provided by Beichen Founder Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). The solutions of different antibiotics (tinidazole, ornidazole, clindamycin, thiamphenicol, penicillin, cefradine, metronidazole, amoxicillin, kanamycin, gentamicin, rutinum, green tea polyphenols, sunitinib, azithromycin, sulfamethoxazole, trimethoprim, clarithromycin, sertraline, uric acid, and tetracycline) were prepared in the concentration of 0.01 M. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solutions with a variety of pH values were obtained by utilizing 1.0 M NaOH or HCl to titrate the solution of 0.02 M disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ.cm) was prepared by the rephile genie water system for all experiments.

The R-CDs were obtained by a one-step solvothermal strategy. First, the accurately weighed 20 mg of resazurin and 50 mg of urea were dissolved in 20 mL of EtOH for ultrasonic dispersion. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the resulting solution was filtered by qualitative filter paper and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, the suspensions were further filtered by 0.22 μM pore diameter microporous membrane and finally, solid-state R-CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation.

The fluorescence assay was conducted in 0.02 mol·L−1 phosphate buffered saline solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) with the concentration of R-CDs determined at 0.06 mg·mL−1. First, 100 μL of prepared R-CDs solution was added to 2 mL PBS solution and then the different concentrations of TCs were introduced individually. The mixed solution was subjected to fluorescence measurements after 2 min and recorded under excitation at 365 nm. All measurements were executed in triplicate.

The fluorescence assay towards different pH was performed in 0.02 mol·L−1 PBS solution. 100 μL of prepared R-CDs solution (0.06 mg·mL−1) was added to 2 mL of different pH solutions. Fluorescence measurements were performed on the mixed solution after 2 min and recorded under excitation at 365 nm. All measurements were executed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: The tetracycline tablets (0.25 g), oxytetracycline tablets (0.125 g), and 20% chlortetracycline hydrochloride soluble powder were purchased from the local drugstore. The sample processing procedure was as follows: Two TC and OTC tables were taken and ground into fine powder in a mortar, respectively. The 0.436 g of TC, 0.457 g of OTC, and 1.03 g of CTC powder were accurately weighed into a 50 ml centrifuge tube with 40 ml of 0.5 M HCl, respectively. Then the mixture was evenly vibrated with ultrasound for 10 min and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm to extract the supernatant. 5 μL of supernatant, 2 mL of PBS solution, and 100 μL of prepared R-CDs solution (0.06 mg·mL-1) were added to 10 mm fluorescence cuvettes and then left standing at room temperature for 2 min, the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded under a fluorescence spectrophotometer. All experiments were measured three times.

The brand of milk was milk deluxe and was acquired from a local convenience store. The proteins in milk were precipitated by using trichloroacetic acid according to early research reports [5]. Briefly, 1 mL of milk containing different concentrations of TCs (15, 25, and 35 μM) was added to a 5 mL centrifuge tube. Then, 2 mL PBS solution (0.02 M, pH = 7) and 0.5 mL of trichloroacetic acid (300 g·L−1) were added to a centrifuge tube. Afterward, the mixed solution was evenly vibrated with ultrasound for 5 min and centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm to remove the proteins. The supernatant was collected and adjusted to neutral through NaOH solution (1 mol·L−1) and the obtained solution was filtered with a 0.22 μM pore diameter microporous membrane. After 2 mL of mixed sample and 100 μL of prepared R-CDs solution (0.06 mg·mL−1) were added to 10 mm fluorescence cuvettes and then left standing at room temperature for 2 min, the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded under a fluorescence spectrophotometer. All experiments were measured three times.

According to transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the morphology of R-CDs possesses a uniform spherical. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the R-CDs sizes considerably emerge between 2.6 nm and 4.4 nm with an average diameter of 3.41 ± 0.02 nm. In addition, the HR-TEM illustrates (right inset of Fig. 1a) that the lattice spacing of R-CDs as 0.22 nm, which is consistent with the (100) plane of graphite-carbon [36]. Furthermore, the functional groups and chemical compositions of R-CDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the absorption bands at around 3317 and 3198 cm−1 be in connection with the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H [37]. The stretching vibrations of C–H emerge around the absorption peaks of 2927 cm−1 [38]. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 1596 and 1383 cm−1 demonstrate the stretching vibrations of CO and C–N [39], respectively. To further investigate the characterization of R-CDs, the XPS spectrum was shown (Fig. 1c). It can be shown that the R-CDs demonstrate three typical peaks of C1s (281.4 eV), O1s (529.3 eV), and N1s (398.5 eV). Furthermore, the high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 1d) show three peaks at 284.3 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.1 eV, corresponding to sp3/sp2 C atoms (C–C/C=O), sp2 ones in C–N/C–O, and sp2 C atoms (COOH) of R-CDs. Moreover, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) demonstrates three characteristic peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.4 eV, and 400.2 eV are in accordance with pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) exhibits two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.5 eV, which relate to C=O and C–O, respectively. The XPS characterization of R-CDs is consistent with the FTIR spectrum, which proves that R-CDs are consisted of aromatic structures with amino and carboxyl groups enriched on the surface.

The optical properties of R-CDs were investigated by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2 a) in the black line illustrates absorption peaks at around 280 nm and 570 nm, and are comparable to the π-π* transition of the sp2 aromatic and the n−π* transition of the CO or C–N, respectively [40–42]. The PL spectra (Fig. 2a) in the blue and red line illustrate that the optimal excitation (λex) and emission(λem) wavelengths of R-CDs are determined as 592 nm and 608 nm. Accordingly, the R-CDs solution exhibits bright red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation (inset of Fig. 2a). As indicated in Fig. 2b, the excitation-emission matrix of R-CDs reveals the excitation-independent characteristic when the excitation wavelength varies from 300 nm to 650 nm, which can be attributed to the uniform surface state and narrow size distribution of R-CDs. Meanwhile, choosing the rhodamine B as reference (QY = 89% in ethanol, λex/λem = 495/515–800), the quantum yield of R-CDs was calculated to be 58.9% (Fig. S1).

In order to evaluate the feasibility of R-CDs in practical applications, the photostability of R-CDs was further investigated. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs scarcely exhibits variation along with the UV irradiation under 365 nm for 60 min (Fig. S2). In addition, the R-CDs remains stable as the concentration of NaCl increased from 0 M to 2 M, which indicates the excellent performance of the R-CDs in the physiological salt environment (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the fluorescence emissions of R-CDs are described with CIE coordinates (0.5833, 0.2832), demonstrating the exceptional optical performance of R-CDs and their tremendous potential for environmental application (Fig. 2c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of R-CDs towards TCs
ce:section.ce:para: It is of considerable importance to develop a simple and sensitive measure for trace detection due to the serious environmental pollution caused by antibiotics. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence response of R-CDs is investigated in the presence of commonly used antibiotics (including tinidazole, ornidazole, clindamycin, thiamphenicol, penicillin, cefradine, metronidazole, amoxicillin, kanamycin, gentamicin, rutinum, green tea polyphenols, sunitinib, azithromycin, sulfamethoxazole, trimethoprim, clarithromycin, sertraline, uric acid, and tetracycline). It exhibits that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at 608 nm decreases in the presence of TCs, while the changes in fluorescence intensity of other competitive antibiotics are negligible. Furthermore, the specific recognition of R-CDs towards TCs (including TC, CTC, and OTC) are investigated in Fig. 4 a–c.

As the concentration of TCs increased, the changes in fluorescence intensity of R-CDs were described in Fig. 4a–c. The emission intensity at 608 nm is linearly decreased with the concentration of TC, CTC, and OTC in the range of 3–40 μM, 4–50 μM, 2–50 μM, and the corresponding linear regression equation are △F = 38.9408C + 166.8087, R2 = 0.9950, △F = 23.2220C + 227.0645, R2 = 0.9960 and △F = 33.0190C + 9.3168, R2 = 0.9948, respectively (ΔF = F–F0, F, and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of the R-CDs at 608 nm in the absence and presence of TCs; where C is the concentration of TCs) (Fig. 4d–f). The detection limits (LODs) of TC, CTC, OTC were calculated as 38.5 nM, 64.6 nM and 45.4 nM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the R-CDs solution exhibits distinct color changes toward TCs under sunlight, illustrating the extraordinary potential of R-CDs for visual identification of TCs (inset of Fig. 4a–c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion of the mechanism of R-CDs detection TCs
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigated the quenching mechanism of this sensitive sensor, UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetimes, and fluorescence spectra were measured. It is predicted that the quenching mechanism of TCs (including TC, CTC, and OTC) may be owing to the inner filtration effect (IFE).

When TCs are mixed into the R-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreases without the fluorescence shift, which is due to the IFE. Generally, the IFE can occur when the UV absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps the excitation spectrum of CDs [52]. As shown in Fig. S5, it is obviously observed that this overlapping part is between TCs and R-CDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs, R-CDs/TC, R-CDs/CTC, and R-CDs/OTC are measured as 3.90 ns, 3.90 ns, 3.95 ns, and 3.99 ns (Fig. S6), this difference less than an order of magnitude, which further verifies the IFE mechanism [49]. These results indicate that the IFE mechanism as a mechanism for the quenching sensor.

The change of pH value is an important factor to predict water quality and physiological determinant of enzymatic activity and biological function, thus the development of nanosensors based on R-CDs to realize optical response to pH is of great significance. As revealed in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs against pH is recorded in the range of 2–13 under excitation at 365 nm. Surprisingly, the R-CDs possess pH-dependent behavior and the PL intensity of the R-CDs dramatically decreases as the pH changes from 7.4 to 4.0 at an emission intensity of 608 nm. Utilizing the nonlinear fitting of the Boltzman function, the pKa is obtained as 5.61 (Fig. 5b). In addition, the fluorescence intensity demonstrates two good linear relationships in the range of 4–5 and 5–6.2 (Fig. 5c–d). The linear regression equation are y = 525.7753x-1564.2603, R2 = 0.9970 and y = 1769.1302x-7929.5460, R2 = 0.9720, respectively. Accordingly, the color of the R-CDs solution changed from yellow to red with increasing pH under sunlight, which proves the application potential of R-CDs for visual response to pH (inset of Fig. 5a).

The R-CDs display a remarkable response to pH and the proposed mechanism can be ascribed to the elementary speciation of nitrogen atoms. According to the high-resolution N1s spectrum R-CDs, there are many nitrogen atoms bonded on the surface of R-CDs in the form of pyridinic-N, amino-N, and pyrrolic-N. The lone pair electrons on the nitrogen atoms could coordinate with H+ in acid solution, demonstrating the pH sensitivity through nitrogen moiety upon protonation or deprotonation. It can be suspected that the combination of H+ with nitrogen atoms on R-CDs results in a significant fluorescent response to pH. As shown in Fig. S7, the anti-interference ability of R-CDs to other acid groups was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs had no obvious change with the addition of other acid groups, such as amino acids, metal cations, and anions, which demonstrates that the R-CDs have specific recognition for hydrogen ions.

In order to further evaluate the practical application value of R-CDs to pH response, the photostability and reversibility of R-CDs towards different pH were investigated. The photostability of R-CDs at different pH (pH = 3.4, 5.6, and 7.4) was measured by recording the fluorescence intensity at λex/λem = 365/608 nm (Fig. 6 a). The results reveal that the R-CDs hold excellent photostability, which can be available for observing pH changes in real-time. Choosing 0.1 M hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide to adjust the pH between 2 and 7 of the R-CDs solution back and forth (Fig. 6b). After five cycles, the pH of the R-CDs solution could be recovered and the fluorescence intensity remains stable. The results demonstrate that the R-CDs have excellent reversibility to pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of TCS in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: For verifying the practical application of the fluorescence sensor, the recovery experiments were conducted by detecting TCs in milk samples, tables, and powder. As shown in Table 3 , when different concentrations of TCs (15, 25, and 35 μM) were added to milk, the samples recoveries are 96.9%–101.7% for TC, 98.6%–102.4% for CTC and 97.7%–101.8% for OTC and RSDs are less than 2.7% (n = 3). As shown in Table 4 , the recovery of tetracycline and oxytetracycline tablets were calculated to be 99.7%–103.7% and 99.9%–103.8% and RSDs are less than 3.9% (n = 3). The recovery of chlortetracycline powder was calculated to be 101.2%–103.1% and RSDs are 0.5% (n = 3). These results demonstrate the applicability and feasibility of this fluorescence monitor based on R-CDs in practical samples.

The multi-functional fluorescence sensor based on R-CDs was designed for sensitive detection of TCs and discrimination of pH. This is the first example of CDs with red-emitting fluorescence compounding the bio-dye of resazurin with small-molecule of urea. The fluorescence of R-CDs can be significantly quenched by TCs at low levels based on the inner filtration effect mechanism accompanied by naked eye color change. More importantly, this method has been successfully achieved the analysis of the concentration of TCs in complicated samples. In addition, the R-CDs revealed a distinct pH-dependent luminescence behavior in the pH range of 4.0–5.0 and 5.0–6.2 with a pKa of 5.61. In summary, the constructed R-CDs provides a novel strategy for long-wavelength CDs and broadens its application in environmental monitoring.

Bingyan Wang: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing-original draft, Writing-review & editing. Changxin Gu: Investigation, Discussed the results. Yuan Jiao: Project administration, Supervision. Yifang Gao: Formal analysis, Discussed the results. Xiaona Liu: Project administration, Supervision. Junmei Guo: Project administration, Supervision. Tianwei Qian: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302124056, No. 202103021223088), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 419071250).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123975.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence and risk assessment of antibiotics in the surface water of Chaohu Lake and its tributaries in China
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhou, G. Liu, M. Arif, X. Shi, S. Wang, Occurrence and risk assessment of antibiotics in the surface water of Chaohu Lake and its tributaries in China, Sci Total Environ, 807 (2022) 151040.

Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.C. Danner, A. Robertson, V. Behrends, J. Reiss, Antibiotic pollution in surface fresh waters: Occurrence and effects, Sci Total Environ, 664 (2019) 793-804.

Crit. Rev. Environ. Sci. Technol.

Anal. Chim. Acta

Mikrochim. Acta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of highly efficient fluorescent carbon dots for tetracycline detection
Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res. Int.
